And so by the time they've invested all that time and energy and effort in filling out the application, not buying is like a waste. The application also has to be structured to make them very aware of how inadequate they are at all sorts of things, therefore needing to be a part of the program. On the second page of the application, you'll find two great questions in the middle. This is, of course, for me, I do marketing. How many marketing strategies or systems do you have in place that consistently generate new business for you? Most people's answer to that, by the way, is zip. How many marketing strategies do you have in place that consistently stimulate repeat business, locking, continuing or renewable income and or stimulate referrals? Guess what most people's number is that they write there? Zero. Yeah, I don't have any of that. And they just got hit in the head with the realization that they should have had those things, and they don't have them. On the next page, there's a question, describe your three major sources of stress, frustration or unhappiness that interfere with your productivity and rob you of peace of mind. All right, so that's, let's make them make themselves unhappy. There's some inadequacy copy on the next page. Rank yourself in direct marketing experience. Very knowledgeable, very knowledgeable, but not very actively implementing. Some are knowledgeable, some are knowledgeable or relative novice. Then there's the, why do you think you are a good candidate for this program essay? You should also note that some of these are essay questions. Some of them are simpler fill-in-the-blank multiple choice questions. Page 137 is a platinum renewal form from O5. The most salient line there is, I am not renewing off from my position to the waiting list. The line off from my position to the waiting list is a very important line. It is again, reminding, reinforcing the picture that there are more people trying to get these chairs than there are chairs. There's an interesting thing that happens with race horses. We will have a horse and we will have let people know we'd like to sell it. Maybe it's in a claiming race and somebody will come to the barn and offer us $3,000 for this $3,000 horse. We were delighted to sell it for $3,000 until somebody offered us $3,000 for it. Then all of a sudden, well if that guy thinks it's worth $3,000, what are we going to replace it with? Another $3,000 horse. Really, if he thinks it's worth $3,000, maybe it's worth $4,000. If you do sell it for $3,000, there's always a lot of regret. Geez, we should have kept it. We should have sold it for $5,000. Maybe he would have paid $4,000. But we were happy to get rid of it until somebody else actually wanted it. The very statement of go ahead, release my seat to the waiting list has the effect of, geez, I was thinking of quitting until everybody else wanted in. Now maybe I don't want to go. Let's see, what else is worth showing? Here we go, let's go along. On page 141, this was a questionnaire application for my wealth group. The rank satisfaction with numbering each item 1 to 10. Again, it's an inadequacy deal. It's making them come to grips with how unsatisfied they are. And the emotional commitment of taking the time to fill all this out. Page 145 of that questionnaire, again, you find a version of what are your top three continuing or nagging, frustrations, fears, anxieties, doubts or problems with your business. Three biggest barriers to easier, more rapid attraction of wealth in your business. They're defining their own problem. Here's a really good one. On 146, if a qualified outside expert was thoroughly evaluating you in the following areas, what grade would he give you? A, B, C, D or F. And then there's all these things they got to grade them on. I actually got this idea from Jim Rohn, because Jim Rohn does the shtick about, if you came and followed somebody around all week at the end of the week, would you be saying, no wonder he's so successful, look at all he does, or no wonder he's not doing so well, look at how little he does, right?
